Press enter or click to view image in full size

Wish I had known about this option much soonerI was debugging a hover effect on the tools cards in Cloud Kingdom, my free app for AI tool discovery, when I stumbled onto a feature in Chrome DevTools that has saved me more time than almost any other inspector trick. It is called Force element state, and if you’ve ever tried to inspect a hover style while your mouse pointer kept stealing the hover state away from the element, you already know the problem it solves.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How do you tweak your hover state without moving your mouse? 🤔The Problem
My fave way to test formats is with Chrome DevTools. I can add new selectors or tweak existing styles until I get the style I want. Then I click the stylesheet link to the right, copy it, and feed it to Claude Code.
Unless it’s a hover state. Until this weekend, when out of sheer desperation I clicked on the :hov button just to see if it could help. Now I'm a believer. 🎶
Let me back up…
Let’s say you want to see the exact CSS applied to a card when someone hovers over it. The instant you move your cursor into the Elements panel to inspect that card, the hover state disappears because your mouse is no longer over the element. 😭 You end up in an awkward dance, hovering with one hand and trying to screenshot or read styles with the other.
The Fix
In the Elements panel, right-click the element you want to inspect and choose Force state, or open the Styles pane and click the :hov button near the top.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Select your element and lock it down by forcing its stateBoth paths open a small panel of checkboxes for pseudo-classes you can lock in place (e.g., :active, :hover, :focus, :focus-within, :focus-visible, :target, and :visited). Check the one you need (I needed :hover). Doing this will cause the browser to hold that state and surface its styles, even after you move the mouse away or click elsewhere in the panel.
DevTools to unwieldy pseudo-classesHere is the :hover state locked on one of the cloud platform cards in Cloud Kingdom. Notice the gold border and background shift are visible without me touching the card at all.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The hover state is on lock even when I move my mouse awayI’ll demo a more pronounced example. I applied this hover effect that sweeps in a new color like Justin Bieber swept his bangs all dramatically as a teen.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A more dramatic exampleNote: If your hover effect doesn’t stick, try selecting a different element in the html (aka DOM).
Once a state is locked, the Styles panel updates in real time, so you can edit values and watch the hovered, focused, or active version of the element change live. That is especially useful when the difference between two states is subtle (e.g., a 2-px transform or a border color shift) because you are not fighting to keep your cursor in place while you compare values.
Other Useful States Beyond Hover
A few of the other pseudo-classes in that same panel are worth knowing.

• :focus shows how an element looks when a user tabs to it with a keyboard, which is helpful in accessibility testing.

• :focus-within applies to a parent element when any child inside it has focus, which is useful for forms where you want a whole field group to highlight.

• :active shows the state during the moment a user is actively clicking, which can be easy to miss because it fires and releases in a fraction of a second.

• :target shows how an element looks when it is the target of a URL fragment (e.g., a jump link to an anchor further down the page).
Not Just a Chrome Feature
Other browsers also offer this feature, but the path to it varies.
Safari
Right-click an element in the DOM view and choose Force Pseudo-Classes from the context menu (learn more).
Firefox
Open the Rules panel in the Inspector and click the :hov button, which opens the same kind of checkbox list for locking pseudo-classes (learn more).
Edge
Edge is built on the same Chromium engine as Chrome, so the Force state panel works identically there.
What About Dynamic States?
If you need to debug dynamic changes, i.e., effects triggered by JavaScript, you need a different approach. I use JavaScript for tooltips all throughout my apps, but I added them to my Model Safety App en masse to keep the UI clutter-free for returning users who need to know if that open model they want to use to save money is actually safe.
Simply select your element in the Elements tab and select Emulate a focused page.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Choose Emulate a focused page for dynamic elementsThis technique is also handy for debugging drop-down menus that rely on focus events rather than CSS pseudo-classes alone.
